text: After lung isolation, the procedure starts with a standard left VATS approach.
We use four laparoscopic trochors and the chest is inflated with CO2 at 8 mmHg.
The visualization of the hernia sac is optimal.
We start by dividing the inferior pulmonary ligament from the hernia sac.
Once the lung is moved medially and superiorly, the hernia sac is dissected posteriorly from the thoracic aorta.
Attention is paid not to injury the contralateral pleura.
Inferiorly, the hernia sac is dissected from its diaphragmatic attachments and anteriorly from the pericardium.
The esophagus is identified at the superior edge of the hernia sac, and a posterior window is created, carefully identifying and sparing the posterior vagus nerve.
A Penrose drain is passed around the esophagus to help with retraction.
The anterior vagus nerve is clearly seen, and some of its bronchial branches are divided in order to allow complete release of the nerve from the other mediastinal structures.
The esophagus is completely mobilized up to the aortic arch and separated from the inferior pulmonary vein, the bronchus, and the main pulmonary artery.
The area sac is subsequently open at the junction with the diaphragm.
This maneuver greatly facilitates crural dissection from the abdominal side.
The sac is then divided circumferentially all the way up to the GE junction, and very careful attention is used at this point to preserve the vagus nerves.
The sac is completely excised and sent to pathology as a specimen.
Adequate esophageal length is verified before ending the thoracic portion of the procedure.
A chest tube is placed and is kept clamped to preserve pneumoperitoneum.
Finally, the lung is re-inflated and the patient is positioned supine for the laparoscopic phase of the procedure.
We use a 5 throw cards laparoscopic approach and the surgeon stands at the patient's right side.
The patient is placed in steep reverse Trendelenburg and a small epigastric incision is made for a Nathanson's liver retractor.
Thanks to the thoracic dissection, the hernia is easily reduced into the abdomen.
The previously placed Penrose drain is grabbed and brought down into the abdominal cavity.
It is clearly visible that the extensive intrathoracic esophageal dissection allowed for a more than adequate length of abdominal esophagus.
The short gastric vessels are divided and the fundus of the stomach is completely mobilized.
The right and left pillars of the diaphragmatic cruise are completely exposed by dissecting the remnants of the hernia sac.
After placing a 52 French bougie into the esophagus, the aieris is re-approximated posteriorly with interrupted non-absorbable sutures.
An anterior stitch is occasionally added for wider defects.
A biological mesh cut into a U-shaped fashion is used to buttress the diaphragmatic repair in an onlay fashion.
The mesh is secured to the diaphragm separately with interrupted sutures and fibrin glue.
The fundus of the stomach is passed behind the esophagus to create a loose and floppy 360 degrees fundoplication.
The wrap is closed anteriorly with three interrupted non-absorbable sutures.
The first two most cephalad stitches include the anterior esophageal wall.
An anterior gastropexy is performed to provide optimal gastric fixation and decrease chances for hernia recurrence.
